behaviors.description: The embedding vector produced for a given query string has the correct dimensionality for the vector database index.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on calling a real embedding model. A mock embedding that always returns the correct dimension would pass even if the real model produces vectors of wrong dimensionality. The failure—real model outputs vector with wrong dimension (e.g., 1024 vs 768)—requires testing against the actual AI/ML model, not a stubbed response. Mocking hides the real failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real embedding model with various query strings (short, long, special characters). For each, verify the output vector has dimensionality matching the vector database schema. Run against actual embedding service and capture metrics on dimension consistency.

behaviors.description: The similarity search returns results ordered by descending cosine similarity score.
behaviors.justification: A mock that returns pre-ordered results would pass even if the real database returns unordered results. To verify the actual database's ordering logic, you need a real (test) vector database instance. The failure—vector DB returning results in wrong order—is a real database behavior that only appears at Integration level with actual vector DB infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a test vector database with known document embeddings. Execute similarity search with a test query vector. Verify returned results are strictly ordered by descending cosine similarity. Test edge cases: identical similarity scores, single result, all results below threshold.

behaviors.description: The LLM re-ranking step changes the order of at least some results compared to the raw similarity ranking when the query has nuanced intent.
behaviors.justification: The re-ranking behavior depends on what the real LLM produces. A mocked LLM response hides the failure where the LLM either doesn't reorder (no-op), reorders incorrectly, or the integration with the LLM fails silently. Testing whether the real LLM actually changes the order based on query semantics requires the actual LLM model, not a canned response.
behaviors.test_description: Create a query with nuanced intent (e.g., 'lightweight waterproof camping gear under $100'). Run embedding → similarity search → real LLM re-ranking. Capture both pre-rerank and post-rerank result lists. Verify at least some positions changed, and that reordering aligns with query semantics (not random).

behaviors.description: A query with no close matches in the database returns an empty result set rather than low-relevance noise.
behaviors.justification: While logic for handling empty results can be unit-tested with mocks, the real failure mode—the real database returning genuinely no matches—only appears at Integration level. A mock returning [] could pass while the real database raises an exception, returns defaults, or produces erratic behavior. Integration testing with a test database that has no matching documents verifies the actual pipeline behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Configure test vector database with documents semantically orthogonal to the test query (e.g., query about cooking tools, database contains astronomy papers). Execute the full search pipeline. Verify API response is an empty list, not padded results, placeholder records, or low-relevance noise.

behaviors.description: The end-to-end latency from receiving the query to returning re-ranked results stays below 2 seconds for the median case.
behaviors.justification: Latency testing cannot succeed at lower levels: unit tests with mocks are orders of magnitude faster and hide real bottlenecks; System/Integration level misses agentic steps. This behavior requires executing the full pipeline—embedding (Agentic), vector search (Integration), and re-ranking (Agentic)—in sequence with real network latency, model inference time, and database I/O. Only Workflow-level testing measures the actual end-to-end latency across all components.
behaviors.test_description: Run complete pipeline with representative queries (vary length, complexity, result set size). Execute 100+ runs. Measure wall-clock latency from query receive to response return. Calculate 50th percentile latency and verify ≤ 2 seconds. Break down latency by component (embedding, search, rerank). Test under realistic load/concurrency.
